_comment: Star Fox franchise entry. All media including canceled/defunct.
description: Nintendo's flagship space-rail shooter franchise (1993) following Fox McCloud and the Star Fox mercenary team defending the Lylat System from the mad scientist Andross. Known for branching flight paths, barrel-roll memes, and the most infamous game development partnership in history.

media.title: Star Fox
media.description: The original Super FX chip-powered rail shooter that founded the franchise — Fox McCloud's first mission against Andross.
media.notes: Requires Super FX chip emulation (bsnes/Mesen-S). The Japanese/PAL version is 'Starwing'.

media.title: Star Fox 2
media.description: Completed sequel shelved in 1995 to avoid upstaging the N64 launch; finally released officially on the SNES Classic Mini in 2017.
media.notes: CANCELED/UNRELEASED at the time — officially released 2017 on SNES Classic. ROM has circulated since the 90s. Super FX2 chip required. Introduces Miyu and Fay as playable characters.

media.title: Star Fox 64
media.description: The definitive entry — full voice acting, branching routes, multiplayer, and the Rumble Pak debut. The gold standard of the franchise.
media.notes: Also released as 'Lylat Wars' in Europe/Australia. Do a barrel roll.

media.title: Star Fox 64 3D
media.description: Full 3DS remake of Star Fox 64 with redrawn assets, gyro controls, and a new multiplayer mode using the 3DS cameras.
media.notes: Best portable version of SF64. Lacks the original's Rumble Pak effects but adds face-cam multiplayer.

media.title: Star Fox Adventures
media.description: Originally Rare's 'Dinosaur Planet' for N64, retrofitted into a Star Fox title — an action-adventure on Sauria with Fox on foot wielding a staff.
media.notes: Divisive. More Zelda than Star Fox. Originally a standalone Rare game; Nintendo mandated the SF skin late in development. Rare's final Nintendo title before the Microsoft acquisition.

media.title: Star Fox: Assault
media.description: Returns to space combat with added on-foot and Landmaster sections; features the Aparoid invasion arc and the death of Pigma Dengar.
media.notes: Rail sections are excellent. On-foot sections weaker. Multiplayer underrated. Namco developed; story picks up after Adventures.

media.title: Star Fox Command
media.description: DS strategy/shooter hybrid with stylus controls and nine branching endings set after the Aparoid war.
media.notes: Nine different endings, most of which are non-canon or contradictory. The canonical ending is ambiguous. Wi-Fi multiplayer no longer functional.

media.title: Star Fox Zero
media.description: Wii U reimagining of SF64 co-developed with PlatinumGames; mandatory dual-screen gyro controls divided audiences sharply.
media.notes: Control scheme is polarizing — TV shows third-person, gamepad shows cockpit view, gyro required. Bundled with Star Fox Guard. Lylat system redesign treated as soft-reboot.

media.title: Star Fox Guard
media.description: Tower-defense spinoff packaged with Star Fox Zero; Slippy's uncle Grippy runs a mining operation you must protect using a network of cameras.
media.notes: Released standalone on eShop and bundled physically with Zero. Light canon tie-in only.

media.title: Star Fox: Super Weekend Competition
media.description: Special competition cartridge used at Nintendo's 1993 retail tournament; a score-attack build of the original Star Fox.
media.notes: COMPETITION CART — not a standalone retail release. Extremely rare physical cartridge. ROM preserved.

media.title: Star Fox (Virtual Boy — Prototype)
media.description: Unreleased prototype for the Virtual Boy — a rail shooter tech demo that never reached public release.
media.notes: CANCELED — prototype ROM leaked. Never publicly released. Would have been a rail-shooter using Virtual Boy's 3D display.

media.title: Dinosaur Planet (Star Fox Adventures Pre-Development)
media.description: Rare's original N64 action-adventure 'Dinosaur Planet' — the game Star Fox Adventures was built from, featuring protagonist Sabre (later replaced by Fox McCloud).
media.notes: UNRELEASED in original form — became Star Fox Adventures. Prototype ROMs leaked in 2021 (both N64 and early GC builds). Playable in Mupen64Plus and Dolphin.

media.title: Star Fox (GBA — Canceled)
media.description: A Star Fox title for Game Boy Advance was announced and shown alongside Adventures but never released.
media.notes: CANCELED — no ROM known to exist. Announced at E3 2002, quietly dropped. Possibly an early concept only.

media.title: Star Fox (Arcade / Miscellaneous appearances)
media.description: Star Fox characters appear as guest fighters in Super Smash Bros. across every entry from Melee (Fox) onward, including Falco, Wolf, and Krystal (as an Assist Trophy).
media.notes: Smash appearances: Melee (2001), Brawl (2008), Wii U/3DS (2014), Ultimate (2018). Not standalone titles but significant franchise presence.

media.title: Star Fox: SNES Prototype (1992 Demo)
media.description: Pre-release prototype build of the original Star Fox used in early press demos; predates the Super FX chip branding.
media.notes: PROTOTYPE — ROM preserved by archivists. Shows early enemy designs and level layouts that differ from the retail release.

media.title: Star Fox: The Battle Begins
media.description: Short CG anime promotional film produced by Production I.G and WIT Studio to promote Star Fox Zero's release.
media.notes: ~4 minutes. Directed by Takahiro Kishida. Available on YouTube/Nintendo's channel. Stunning theatrical-quality animation; not canon to the games.

media.title: Star Fox: Farewell, Beloved Falco
media.description: Official Nintendo manga bridging the gap between Star Fox 64 and Star Fox Adventures, explaining why Falco left the team.
media.notes: Published by Nintendo in their official media channels. Fills the 8-year timeline gap. Out of print; fan scanlations available.

media.title: Nintendo Power Star Fox Comics
media.description: A 6-part comic series published in Nintendo Power magazine alongside the original SNES game's launch, written and drawn by Benimaru Itoh.
media.notes: Western publication. Ran in Nintendo Power issues 1993–1994. Adapts and expands the original game's story with pre-N64 character designs. Collected in 'The Official Nintendo Player's Guide'. Non-canon.

media.title: Star Fox manga (Ikue Itoh / Nintendo Power Japan)
media.description: Japanese manga adaptation of the original Star Fox published in Nintendo's official Japanese publications.
media.notes: Separate from the western Nintendo Power comics. Features different art and story direction.

media.title: Star Fox 64 manga (CoroCoro Comic)
media.description: CoroCoro Comic manga adaptation of Star Fox 64 published around the game's Japanese launch.
media.notes: Part of CoroCoro's ongoing Nintendo game adaptation series. Out of print.
